citation.title: New insights on the organic fouling mechanism of ultrafiltration membranes using hybrid QCM-D–LSPR

content: A B S T R A C T Organic fouling of ultrafiltration (UF) membranes is a major drawback and therefore, a rigorous analysis of the interactions of macromolecules with the outer membrane surface as well as with the confined porous membrane structure is required. This work provides new insights into the interplay between the interactions and confor­mation of alginate, a model organic foulant, as it penetrates the porous structure of an UF membrane. Alginate, like other organic foulants, can adopt conformations and orientations on the membrane surface that change in response to the aqueous conditions. In this work, adsorbed alginate layers were tested on a membrane-mimetic sensor surface using a hybrid system consisting of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) sensing and quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation monitoring (QCM-D). The alginate conformation and adhesion on the sensor were consistent with the effects of alginate penetrating to the UF membrane pores affecting permeability at various ionic strengths. To study the interactions and conformational changes of alginate on the surface, the membrane surface and the hybrid sensor were given a positive charge by modifying them with species bearing primary amine groups, 2-aminoethyl methacrylate and 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane, respectively.

Intriguingly, on the pristine surfaces, increasing ionic strength induced a reversible increase in alginate areal density as measured by LSPR, indicating changes in alginate conformation, which increased the effective UF membrane pore diameter and increased membrane permeability. In contrast, the modified membranes with positive surfaces did not exhibit these changes in alginate conformation caused by ionic strength and membrane permeability showed no response to the aqueous ionic strength. This novel analysis of foulant conformation on a membranemimetic LSPR sensor, was further confirmed by the standard pore blocking model. QCM-D analysis revealed the expected responses to surface charge and ionic strength, i.e., the alginate layer’s viscoelasticity increased on the positively charged surface and with increasing ionic strength. These analyses at the nanometer scale provide critical mechanistic insight into the way fouling reduces UF membrane performance.

citation.title: New insights on the organic fouling mechanism of ultrafiltration membranes using hybrid QCM-D–LSPR

content: 1. Introduction Ultrafiltration (UF) membranes have gained considerable attention, due to their widespread use in drinking, domestic, and industrial wastewater treatment. Besides their physicochemical stability and me­chanical strength, their ability to operate at low pressure means they require lower energy inputs than other membrane treatment process to yield high-quality water [1–3]. However, like other membrane pro­cesses, UF membranes suffer deteriorating permeability and selectivity over time because of fouling by various substances [4,5]. Fouling in­volves rejected materials (e.g., suspended solids and colloids from the filtrate) accumulating on the membrane surface and within its pores, thus reducing its performance and lifetime [6–8]. Increasing membrane hydraulic resistance followed by frequent chemical cleaning cycles consequently reduces the UF membrane lifespan [9]. During filtration, different mechanisms govern fouling according to the rejection, trans­port, and interactions of the foulants with the outer membrane surface and within its porous structure [10,11]. The foulants can be partially adsorbed within pores (pore constriction or standard blocking); completely plug the pore (complete blocking); or accumulate on the membrane surface (intermediate blocking or cake formation) [12]. Fouling commonly involves a combination of these mechanisms.

Foulants usually adsorb to a membrane surface via hydrophobic in­teractions, electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding, or van der Waal’s attraction. Therefore, understanding these interactions is important to revealing the nature of a foulant’s adsorption to a mem­brane, and thus to developing effective strategies for cleaning and mitigation. In addition to the adsorption of foulants to a membrane surface, constriction of membrane pores by organic molecules pene­trating them leads to irreversible fouling. Some attempts to reduce the fouling of UF membranes have focused on modifying the membrane surface charge [15]; however, it is generally accepted that fouling can be reduced by increasing the surface hydrophilicity. This can be done by giving the surface a neutral charge and H-bond accepting groups, and by avoiding H-bond donating groups [16–18]. Therefore, the effects of the hydrophobicity and surface charge of both the foulants and membrane on the fouling process have been extensively studied [19–25].

citation.title: New insights on the organic fouling mechanism of ultrafiltration membranes using hybrid QCM-D–LSPR

content: , the effects of the hydrophobicity and surface charge of both the foulants and membrane on the fouling process have been extensively studied [19–25]. Although avoiding membrane surface–foulant adsorption interactions has often been considered [12,26], the effects of trapped foulants and their conformation and hydration in the confined porous environment of an UF membrane has rarely been reported. Changes to the aqueous environment, such as varying the ionic strength, pH, and presence of multi-valent cations, can affect a foulant’s interactions with the membrane, hydration, and conformation, owing to variations in the effectiveness of electrostatic forces and other types of interactions as described by DLVO theory [27,28]. Surface charge can affect the hydration layer around an adsorbed foulant, affecting its transport in the membrane. Changes in the electrostatic properties of the foulant can alter its conformation, which can affect its entrapment or adsorption in the confined porous structure of the membrane [29,30].

citation.title: New insights on the organic fouling mechanism of ultrafiltration membranes using hybrid QCM-D–LSPR

content: The fouling mechanisms of UF membranes are commonly assessed by using mathematical modelling [11,31] of permeate flux variations to distinguish the effects of pore constriction from other ways of clogging the membrane [32,33]. An in-situ measurement of electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) can differentiate between fouling at the external surface and the porous UF membrane confined environment [34]. This is mostly helpful to assess what part of the porous membrane is clogged, providing critical information for cleaning strategies. Assessing the behavior of adsorbed foulants on a membrane surface, including their conformation and hydration, requires advanced analysis. An emerging method for assessing membrane fouling mechanisms is quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation monitoring (QCM-D), which has been used to study interactions related to soft and solvent interfaces that are directly relevant to membrane fouling mechanisms [35]. QCM-D results showed that the frequency shifts at different overtones appear unrelated to UF membrane fouling, while other out­puts, including the fouling layer’s adsorbed hydrated mass and visco­elastic properties, provided clear relationships [30,36–41]. The adsorption of macromolecules on a QCM-D sensor always in­volves associated water molecules, leading to a hydrated matrix with a certain swelling capability at the interface.

content: cules on a QCM-D sensor always in­volves associated water molecules, leading to a hydrated matrix with a certain swelling capability at the interface. The viscoelastic properties of an adsorbed hydrated layer affect the inertial, viscous, and elastic loadings on the sensor, which affect the shifts of resonance frequency Δf and dissipation ΔD. This can lead to inconsistent conclusions being drawn regarding the amount of adsorbed material and thus about the associated effects on membrane fouling, even for sensors coated with membrane material [42–44]. To avoid common sources of inconsistent conclusions from QCM-D results and incorrect predictions about mem­brane fouling, a new technology has been developed, combining local­ized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) sensing with QCM-D to study reverse osmosis and UF membrane fouling [30,45]. Journal of Membrane Science 725 (2025) 124044 sensing technology that detects changes in the local dielectric environ­ment near the surface by measuring shifts in the light extinction peak, caused by collective electron charge oscillations in noble metal nano­structures. LSPR has a high spatial resolution, limited only by the size of the noble metal nanostructures. Small changes in the local dielectric environment can be used to analyze adsorption interactions of a variety of organic and inorganic macromolecules as well as associated conformational changes of the adsorbate. Taking advantage of LSPR, a potential next-generation smart fouling prediction can be developed as well as fundamental under­standing of fouling mechanisms. The use of hybrid QCM-D–LSPR for membrane fouling studies offers the advantage of being able to measure an adsorbed layer at an interface at different distances, of up to 300 nm for QCM-D [46] and up to 30 nm for most LSPR sensors [47]. This combination allows comprehensive characterization of the adsorbed layer, providing information about the hydrated mass, adhesion strength, and viscoelastic properties from QCM-D, and about the dry mass and conformation from LSPR.

In this study, we used QCM-D–LSPR hybrid surface analysis to assess for the first time, the relation between adsorbed foulant conformation in the confined porous structure of a UF membrane and its effect on membrane permeability. To study pore-clogging fouling, we used very low viscosity (VLV) alginate as a model foulant. The foulant (125 ± 4.8 kDa) was smaller than the pol­yether sulfone (PES) membrane’s molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 300 kDa. As a control experiment, a pristine PES membrane was modified with primary amine groups to give the surface a positive charge, induce electrostatic attraction between the alginate and the membrane’s porous structure, and reduce conformation upon changes in the aquatic condition. The evolution of the alginate fouling layer’s conformation and its interaction (attachment and detachment) with a surface with similar chemistry to the membrane was tested using a QCM-D–LSPR flow cell hosting a new QCM-D–LSPR UF membrane-mimetic sensor. The results demonstrate the importance of a foulant’s conformation, rather than just its affinity to the membrane, to the foulant retention and clogging membrane pores’ structure. 2. A pristine PES UF membrane (300 kDa MWCO, Synder Filtration, USA) was UV-irradiated for graft polymeri­zation using a 0.5 % (w/v) solution of 2-aminoethyl methacrylate hy­drochloride (AEMA; Polysciences Inc., USA). Before grafting, the membrane was soaked in 50 % (v/v) ethanol/water mixture for 24 h to remove any noncovalently bonded substances and preservatives. The grafting solution was purged with N2 gas for 10 min for oxygen removal. Grafting was then performed for 10 min under UV light of 67.6 mW/cm2 (IntelliRay 400 UV, Uvitron, USA) intensity. Membrane characterization. Zeta potential (SurPass, Anton Paar, Austria) tests in 1 mM KCl at pH 3–8.5 on both the pristine and AEMAmodified PES membranes confirmed the presence of a positively charged grafted layer on the AEMA-modified PES membrane. A permeability test with double distilled water (DDW) was performed and repeated at least three times. Captive bubble contact angle measurements (OCA 200, DataPhysics, Germany) to determine membrane hydrophobicity were conducted with 6 μL of air at pH 4, 7, and 10 in pH buffer solutions (Carlo Erba, Italy). The MWCO of the PES and AEMA membranes was evaluated based on 90 % rejection of [48] 1 g/L polyethylene oxide (PEO; Sigma-Aldrich, USA) molecular weights of 100, 200, and 400 kDa solubilized in DDW.

A positively charged surface was prepared using 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES; Sigma-Aldrich, USA), selected for its similarity to the AEMA monomer coating that provided the PES membrane with a positive charge. Both APTES and AEMA monomers possess primary amine (–NH2) groups. APTES can easily undergo silanization to form siloxane (Si–O–Si) bonds linking it to a SiO2-coated sensor [52]. The sensors were exposed for 4 h to a 1 % (v/v) solution of APTES in absolute ethanol. To create a PES membrane-mimetic sensor, PES was coated over the APTES layer, with APTES serving as a support and foundation for the PES coating. A PES layer was spin-coated (WS-400BZ-6NPP/LITE, Laurell Technologies, USA) at 4000 rpm for 30 s on APTES using a 0.5 % (w/v) solution of PES (80 μL, Ultrason E 6020P, BASF, USA) in dichloromethane. We used a positively charged control membrane as well as positively charged control QCM-D – LSPR sensor that provided strong electrostatic attraction with the alginate, hence altering its conformation. A membrane surface bearing positively charged moieties has a strong electrostatic attraction with alginate and can screen the effects of aqueous chemistry, elongating or collapsing the alginate and affecting the way alginate clogged the confined environ­ment of the UF membrane pores. Fig. 1A shows the contact angle of pristine and AEMA-modified membranes at different pH values. A small reduction in the air bubble contact angle, indicating a slight increase in surface hydrophobicity, was detected as pH increased to 10; the contact angle was 148.5◦± 2◦at pH 4 and 145.3◦± 0.7◦at pH 7, and decreased to 136.6◦± 1.4◦at pH 10. This result can be explained by the depro­tonation of the AEMA amine group at pH 10, whereas protonation of the amine group maintained the surface polarity around pH neutral and below. As expected, the pristine PES UF membrane, made from nonprotonated material and stable to pH variation, did not show a similar trend in air bubble contact angle as pH increased, when no significant change in contact angle was detected.

The PES membrane consistently exhibited a negative net surface charge across all tested pH ranges. This negative charge mainly resulted from the presence of sulfonate groups in the membrane struc­ture [58,59]. In contrast, the AEMA-modified membrane displayed a variation in net charge with an isoelectric point, showing a positive net charge at low pH and a negative net charge at high pH. This behavior was likely due to the protonation and deprotonation of the primary amine group of AEMA, confirming the presence of a positively charged surface layer owing to modification [60]. The AEMA modified versus pristine PES membrane show improved rejection of PEO with MWCO of ~225 kDa vs. ~350 kDa, respectively, attributed to the modified AEMA layer (Fig. 1C). Furthermore, HR-SEM analysis visually showed smaller pore diameter distribution after AEMA modification layer (Fig. 1D). XPS analysis was performed to verify the successful modification of the PES UF membrane with UV-grafted AEMA further. The pristine PES and AEMA-modified surfaces were scanned to identify changes in chemical groups of interest (C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p; Table 1 and Fig. S5).

However, this prediction was not straightforward as increasing ionic strength can weaken the electrostatic repulsion between the alginate and the PES membrane due to charge screening (Hu et al., 2019 [67] and Fig. 3A). Prior to the observation on the effect of ionic strength on membrane fouling as well as conformation on top of LSPR-membrane mimetic surface, indirect analysis of the alginate conformational changes was analyzed by DLS, revealing a shift in the hydrated aggre­gate size distribution profile toward smaller aggregates upon elevation of the ionic strength (Fig. 3 B). The reduction in charge screening of the alginate at lower ionic strength (Fig. 3 A) will likely contribute to extended conformation of alginate molecules forming bigger aggregates as shown in Fig. 3B. Membrane fouling results are provided as the ratio of TMP to initial TMP, (TMP/TMP0). For both membranes, after acquiring a constant TMP baseline with a 100 mM NaCl background solution, filtration of alginate solution in 100 mM NaCl provided a fouling scenario tested as elevation of normalized TMP (Fig. 4A). It should be mentioned that alginate adsorption to AEMA is enhanced by its positive charge, how­ever, adsorption could be limited by the presence of competing chloride anions. Intriguingly, greater fouling occurred on the pristine PES membrane than on the AEMA-modified membrane (TMP/TMP0 of ~1.4 versus ~1.3, Fig. 4A and repeated in Fig.

content: , greater fouling occurred on the pristine PES membrane than on the AEMA-modified membrane (TMP/TMP0 of ~1.4 versus ~1.3, Fig. 4A and repeated in Fig. S8). In addition, both mem­branes showed similarly high alginate rejection (85.8 ± 2.2 % and 85.4 ± 1.8 %, respectively), indicating high adsorption of alginate onto the membranes’ porous structure and comparable amounts of alginate accumulation on both membranes. The lower fouling of the AEMA membrane compared to the pristine PES membrane is likely affected by alginate conformation on membrane surface further discussed in the QCM-D – LSPR analysis part. After the first stage of alginate fouling and washing with 100 mM NaCl background solution (Fig. 4A and 30–150 min), both membranes showed similar TMP/TMP0 values of ~1.2. The effects of possible algi­nate hydration and conformation on the increase in TMP were analyzed by altering the ionic strength via filtering a series of NaCl solutions through the fouled membrane (Fig. 4A and 150–440 min). Between 150 and 310 min, as the filtered solution alternated between 10 and 100 mM NaCl, the AEMA-modified membrane showed almost no change in TMP, whereas the PES membrane showed an increase in TMP/TMP0 when the ionic strength was reduced to 10 mM and a decrease of similar magni­tude as it rose to 100 mM NaCl [68].

content: brane showed an increase in TMP/TMP0 when the ionic strength was reduced to 10 mM and a decrease of similar magni­tude as it rose to 100 mM NaCl [68]. Following the idea that decreasing ionic strength elongates the molecular conformation, interesting fouling behavior of alginate was observed in the PES membrane as the back­ground solution alternated between 10 mM NaCl and DDW. Once the fouled membrane filtered DDW, alginate in the porous structure reduced its permeability, and permeability recovered in 10 mM NaCl (Fig. 4A and 310–440 min). To provide more evidence of a reduction in ionic strength hydrating and elongating alginate, and thus influencing alginate fouling, fouling experiments with the pristine PES and AEMA-modified membranes were conducted with VLV alginate in DDW (Fig. 4B). The substantially lower fouling of the modified membrane (TMP/TMP0 of ~1.2 vs. ~2.0 for the pristine PES) and its lower alginate rejection (59.8 % vs. 92.6 % for pristine PES) provide a further perception of a thinner, more rigid alginate layer accumulating in the AEMA membrane structure compared to the pristine UF membrane despite alginate’s stronger adsorption to the positively charged modified surface than to the pristine PES mem­brane. The lower rejection of alginate by the AEMA membrane and its lower fouling magnitude take place in spite of the smaller pores’ radius of AEMA compared to the pristine membrane.

Studying alginate adsorption and conformation on a membranemimetic surface using QCM-D–LSPR: LSPR analysis The hybrid testing procedure allowed detailed investigation of the alginate–surface interactions, which explained the effect of alginate dynamic conformation in the porous membrane structure on the mem­brane’s permeability. The dry molecular mass of the adsorbed alginate layer was characterized by LPSR (Fig. 5A). The alginate dry mass adsorbed on the PES-coated QCM-D–LSPR sensor was slightly smaller than on the positively charged APTES-modified sensor; both surfaces showed similar removal rates of adsorbed alginate during the 100 mM NaCl washing stage (Fig. 5A and 30–150 min). This resembles the trends during the initial UF membrane fouling (Fig. 4A and 30–190 min). The adsorbed dry mass results, assessed by LSPR from 190 to 420 min showed that the APTES-coated sensor maintained a relatively constant adsorbed mass close to the LSPR active surface (i.e., the AuNPs), regardless of changes in the aqueous conditions. This behavior was probably due to strong adsorption of the negatively charged alginate backbone to the APTES-positive charge coating. In contrast, for the PEScoated surface, the shifts in the local adsorbed dry mass were reversed as the ionic strength varied with the alternating 10 and 100 mM NaCl; this was accompanied by a continuous decrease in the adsorbed dry mass of alginate.

content: the ionic strength varied with the alternating 10 and 100 mM NaCl; this was accompanied by a continuous decrease in the adsorbed dry mass of alginate. These observations were attributed to weak hydrophobic in­teractions between alginate and the PES surface. The adsorbed dry mass of alginate decreased with decreasing ionic strength as the NaCl solution concentration changed from 100 to 10 mM, and then it increased with the increasing ionic strength as the solution concentration returned to 100 mM. The areal adsorbed mass density, Γs (ng/cm3), was affected by charge screening that reduced the alginate zeta potential at high ionic strength (Fig. 3A). The elevation in the adsorbed areal mass concen­tration on the PES-coated sensor as ionic strength was elevated to 100 mM was attributed to change in the conformation of alginate, in spite of the general desorption trend of alginate from this sensor, which is likely due to the weak hydrophobic interactions between alginate and the PES surface. We still recognize a reversibility of the decreases and increases of Γs (ng/cm3) between 150 and 310 min as the background solution alternated between 100 and 10 mM NaCl with a general desorption trend of alginate from the sensor. Elevation of the local alginate con­centration without addition of alginate to the LSPR sensor, as ionic strength is elevated (Fig. 5 A), must come from the adsorbed alginate and compaction of alginate close to the surface.

In a similar manner, without addition of alginate to the fouled UF membrane, permeability is elevated (TMP/TMP0 is reduced) as ionic strength is elevated (Fig. 4 A) due to compaction of alginate inside the porous structure and increase of the effective radius for water transport. The relatively constant perme­ability of the AEMA-modified membrane after introducing alginate to the membrane, even when DDW was used to wash the membrane (Fig. 4A), is likely because a stable adsorbed alginate on the APTES-modified porous structure, strongly interacting with the positively charged membrane surface. This was approved by testing alginate interaction on the APTES-modified LSPR sensor, showing a relatively constant and high local concentration of adsorbed alginate, regardless of the changes in aquatic conditions (Fig. 5A). Hence, modifying both membrane porous structure and LSPR sensor avoided conformational changes of alginate in proximity with the surface, by the strong electrostatic attraction be­tween alginate and the positive charge modified surface. 3.4.

Studying alginate adsorption, hydration, and viscoelastic properties on a membrane-mimetic surface using QCM-D–LSPR: QCM-D analysis QCM-D analysis of the effects of the adsorbed layer on shifts in the resonance frequency and dissipation energy provided information about the layer’s hydrated thickness and viscoelastic properties. During algi­nate adsorption stage (Fig. 5 B&C, 30–150 min), a slightly higher fre­quency shift for alginate adsorption was observed for the positively charged, APTES-coated sensor, which also showed lower dissipation

These results corroborate with the hy­pothesis that the positively charged surface promotes adsorption and formation of a rigid layer with high shear modulus and shear viscosity, as described further in the Kelvin–Voigt based viscoelastic modelling results (Fig. 6 B&C). The APTES-coated sensor had a similar layer thickness to the one of the PES-coated sensor at the beginning of the experiment, during the adsorption stage (Fig. 6A, 30–150 min). As the experiment progressed, the layer thickened more on the positively charged surface than on the PES-coated surface, due to washing of alginate material from the PES-coated sensor. The hydrated alginate thickness assessed by QCM-D reversibly increased with decreasing ionic strength (as the solution changed from 100 to 10 mM NaCl and then to DDW); this parallels a minor but reversible decrease in the alginate areal concentration, Γs, as determined by LSPR sensing (Fig. 5A). These results clearly indicate that the hydration of alginate layer, as observed within the QCM-D probe depth, affects its shear modulus and shear viscosity (Fig. 6B and C).

content: ndicate that the hydration of alginate layer, as observed within the QCM-D probe depth, affects its shear modulus and shear viscosity (Fig. 6B and C). Note that higher shear viscosity and elasticity were shown to elevate the resistance of fouling layer to hydraulic cleaning due to their stronger mechanical properties [69]. It is likely that the positively charged surface supports the mechanical properties of the adsorbed alginate layer. For both PES and APTES coatings, changing the 10 mM NaCl solution to DDW reduced the frequency shift (Fig. 5B), owing to the reductions in the alginate layer’s viscosity (Fig. 6B) and elasticity (Fig. 6C). Transient increases occurred in the sensor’s dissipation shift (at 310 and 390 min) as the solution changed from 10 mM NaCl to DDW. The reductions in the viscosity and elasticity of the alginate layer were consistent with the expected increase in the hydrated thickness (Fig. 6A). The increased hydration (Fig. 6A) coupled with reduced dry mass alginate concen­tration (Fig. 5A) in the presence of DDW results from molecular chain elongation as indicated by the QCM-D and LSPR, respectively, as ionic strength decreased. Such conformational changes eventually clog the porous structure of the UF membrane and increase its resistance to water flow. The hydrated thickness also increased on the APTES-coated QCMD–LSPR sensor (Fig.

The hydrated thickness also increased on the APTES-coated QCMD–LSPR sensor (Fig. 6A); however, the local concentrations measured by LSPR showed that the APTES-coated sensor accommodated a more stable and compact adsorbed mass than the adsorbed layer on the PEScoated sensor (Fig. 5A). The increase in the hydrated thickness on the APTES-coated sensor could be attributed to penetration of water mole­cules among the alginate molecules, despite their interactions with the surface. Interestingly, the alginate layer on the PES-coated sensor showed high hydration capacity (Fig. 6A) relative to the small amount of alginate remaining on the sensor (Fig. 5A). Hence, we can conclude that the high hydration capacity of alginate that corresponds with this biopolymer elongation can significantly affect pore clogging, even for relatively small amount of material that is retained by the porous membrane structure. 4. Conclusions A hybrid acoustic and nanoplasmonic study assessed the adsorption, conformation, hydration, and viscoelastic properties of alginate on a UF membrane-mimetic QCM-D–LSPR sensor. The sensor was fabricated by coating a pristine SiO2 coated QCM-D–LSPR sensor with APTES by silanization, to allow further spin-coating with PES on top of the APTESmodified SiO2 layer.

content: ng a pristine SiO2 coated QCM-D–LSPR sensor with APTES by silanization, to allow further spin-coating with PES on top of the APTESmodified SiO2 layer. The fouling mechanisms of confined porous envi­ronments are commonly tested by modelling approaches and analysis of the rejected fouling components, whereas this study directly linked the UF membrane’s performance to the conformation, hydration, and adhesion of a model foulant on the membrane mimetic surface. The hybrid QCM-D–LSPR membrane-mimetic sensor provided combined data for the conformation of macromolecules up ~30 nm from the surface and the mechanical load of the fouling layer up to ~300 nm from the surface. The fouling layer’s hydration and conformation, as deter­mined by LSPR, agree with its viscoelastic properties obtained from the

citation.title: New insights on the organic fouling mechanism of ultrafiltration membranes using hybrid QCM-D–LSPR

content:
QCM-D results using the Kelvin–Voigt viscoelastic vibration isolator model. Counterintuitively, aqueous conditions that reduced adhesion of the foulant affected its conformation in such a way that eventually worsened its clogging of the membrane’s porous structure. These results explore the role of a penetrated foulant of a size of the same order of magnitude as the membrane pores and the consequent effects of the interplay between the foulant’s conformation and interactions with the porous surface. The QCM-D–LSPR sensor provided direct evidence for the adverse effects of foulant conformation on pore clogging and thus on the permeability of a UF membrane. When we consider assessing membrane backwashing efficiency under conditions of foulant swelling we need to evaluate fouling layer removability. New avenues for membrane fouling research should use QCM-D–LSPR technology to exploit its potential for monitoring and predicting fouling and devel­oping new antifouling membrane surfaces.
